title: FINS3616 Week 3 Updated
body:
FINS3616 INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS FINANCE


                     LECTURE 3
      INTERNATIONAL PARITY CONDITIONS


       Janghoon Shon (University of New South Wales)
 ILAB TUTORIALS -- ILAB ASSIGNMENT

Format:             Moodle submission
Due:                Friday, July 18th, 17:00
% Evaluation:       15%
The task of the project is to forecast foreign exchange rates using regression models and to understand how to use
forecast information.
Each student is required to attend an iLab session in Week 3 or Week 4 in order to learn how to use the FACTSET
Database to access historical exchange rate data and macroeconomic information. Students are required to
download historical data from the FACTSET database and answer the list of assigned questions.
Each student will submit an individual assignment online through Moodle at the end of Week 7. The specific details
of the project, as well as the specific assessment criteria, will be available on Moodle by Week 4. Students are also
expected to conduct their own research for this assignment.


                                                                                                                       2
 MID-TERM EXAM

Format:            Online
Time:              Saturday, June 28th, 1400-1500 with a 15-minute-buffer
Question types:    Single choices and numerical responses (fill-in blanks)
Duration:          1 hour
Coverage:          Week 1 – Week 4
% Evaluation:      20%


Let me know if you have issues with the exam time.




                                                                                3
 MOCK MID-TERM EXAM

Format:             Online
Time:               from Wednesday, June 18th, 1400
Question types:     Single choices and numerical responses (fill-in blanks)
Duration:           30 minutes
Coverage:           Week 1 – Week 4
% Evaluation:       0%


Good practice, mid-term will have a similar type of questions, but potentially more challenging questions.




                                                                                                                4
 PLAN

W1: Introduction to International finance & exchange rates
W2: Description of FX markets & analysis of exchange rate risk
W3: International parity conditions
W4: Exchange rate systems and fundamentals
W5: Exchange rate trading and forecasting
    W6: Flexi Week
W7: Foreign currency derivatives
W8: FX risk hedging and management within multinationals
W9: International corporate issues
W10: International financing: cross-listing and Eurobonds

                                                                    5
 TODAY

1) Purchasing power parity


2) Uncovered interest rate parity
•   Forward premium


3) Valuation model with monetary shock


4) Forecasting risk of exchange rate devaluations




                                                       6
       / Introduction to international finance /




PURCHASING POWER PARITY
 PURCHASING POWER PARITY (PPP)

Absolute Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
Idea: The same good is at the same price everywhere (law of one price).
    •   The exchange rate then equals the consumer price ratio
    •   Simplifying assumptions:
         •   No transportation costs
         •   Same basket of goods in all countries




                                                                             8
 PURCHASING POWER PARITY (PPP)

Absolute Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
Imagine that Australia and the USA produce only potatoes.
The cost of a potato is 1 USD in the U.S. and 2 AUD in Australia.


                                   What should the AUD/USD exchange rate be?




                                                                                  9
 PURCHASING POWER PARITY (PPP)

Absolute Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)

                Consider the following Big Mac Burger sold in period t:




            •   Since the two burgers are identical, their respective currency-adjusted prices should be identical.
            •   Thus, we can derive the exchange rate between the US dollar and the Australian dollar:

                        𝑈𝑆𝐷7.25                                             𝐴𝑈𝐷9.75
                                = 0.7436 𝑈𝑆𝐷/𝐴𝑈𝐷                 or                 = 1.3348 𝐴𝑈𝐷/𝑈𝑆𝐷
                        𝐴𝑈𝐷9.75                                             𝑈𝑆𝐷7.25
                                                                                                                         10
 PURCHASING POWER PARITY (PPP)

Relative Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
Idea: We consider price growth (inflation) instead of price levels
    •   Expected exchange rate growth = inflation differential
    •   Same simplifying assumptions as absolute PPP
                                               𝐸 𝑆𝑡+1    1 + Π𝑈𝑆𝐷
                                                       =
                                                   𝑆𝑡    1 + Π𝐴𝑈𝐷
                                            𝐸 Δ𝑆𝑡+1
                                                      ≈ Π𝑈𝑆𝐷 − Π𝐴𝑈𝐷
                                                𝑆𝑡
    with S=USD/AUD (units of USD for 1 AUD) and Π = inflation




                                                                         11
 PURCHASING POWER PARITY (PPP)

Long-term fundamental valuation
•   Useful in the long term
•   Not very effective in the short term
•   See Grossman & Rogoff (1995), Lothian & Taylor (1996)


Common Consumer Price Index Alternatives:
•   Big Mac Index (The Economist)
•   IKEA Billy Bookshelf Index (Bloomberg)




                                                               12
 PURCHASING POWER PARITY (PPP)




                                Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2017)      13
 PARITY RELATIONS

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) in practice


                                Which currencies are over/undervalued?




                                                                            14
 PARITY RELATIONS




Source: https://fx.sauder.ubc.ca/PPP.php      15
 PARITY RELATIONS

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
Widely used in practice
    •   For example:
     “PPP gives you a framework as to where currencies are trading in the bigger picture, said Ian Stannard, senior
currency strategist at BNP Paribas”
         Reuters, 25 May 2010, « Euro seen heading for parity with dollar»


                                               Does it really work?




                                                                                                                     16
   PARITY RELATIONS




Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2012)

   PARITY RELATIONS




Source: Bekaert and Hodrick (2012)

   PARITY RELATIONS




Source: https://fx.sauder.ubc.ca/PPP.php




                                              19
 PARITY RELATIONS

Relation between exchange rate changes and relative PPP

                                                 The regression line shows a strong long-run
                                                 relation between relative changes in inflation and
                                                 changes in exchange rates.

                                                 Data from 1981-2017.




                                                                                                         20
 PARITY RELATIONS

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP): Investment application
•   Menkhoff, Sarno, Schmeling, and Schrimpf (2017) investigate currency value strategies.
•   They construct a trading strategy based on relative PPP deviations.
•   “Building portfolios to mimic the returns to a currency value investment strategy allows for a straightforward
    assessment of the economic significance of the link between real exchange rates, fundamentals, and currency risk
    premiums.”




                                                                                                                      21
 PARITY RELATIONS

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP): Investment application
•   Sort currencies into portfolios based on 5-year change of PPP
•   Portfolio 1 = 25% of currencies that are most overvalued
•   Portfolio 4 = 25% of currencies that are most undervalued


Result:
•   Find predictive power for the cross-section of currency excess returns.
•   Profitable long-short strategy (buy P4 and sell P1)




                                                                                 22
        / Introduction to international finance /




UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY
 UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY (UIP)

Uncovered interest rate parity (UIP)
Hypothesis that the expected return on the uncovered (=unhedged) foreign money market investment equals the
known return from investing in risk-free rates internationally.


Idea
       The expected growth of the exchange rate depends on the interest rate differential:


                                                𝐸 𝑆𝑡+1   1 + 𝑟𝐴𝑈𝐷
                                                       =
                                                  𝑆𝑡     1 + 𝑟𝑈𝑆𝐷
       with S=AUD/USD (AUD for 1 USD) and r = interest rate


                                                                                                             24
 UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY (UIP)

Intuition
•   Interest rate differential across countries on bonds of similar credit risk should reveal an expectation of currency
    depreciation.
•   Initially attractive interest rate differential should be met by an offsetting FX move, so that you make no more
    money on average in one currency versus another.


Note
•   The ex post realized exchange rate St+1 will not necessarily be equal to E(St+1), which is the rate expected on
    average.




                                                                                                                          25
 UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY (UIP)

Uncovered interest rate parity (UIP)
More formally,


                                       𝑆𝑡+1
                                  𝐸         1 + 𝑟𝑈𝑆𝐷 − (1 + 𝑟𝐴𝑈𝐷 ) = 0
                                        𝑆𝑡



    •The zero-investment (zero-cost) portfolio of money market instruments should yield 0 in
    expectation if investors are rational and risk-neutral.

    •This assumes free capital mobility and the absence of taxes on capital transfers.




                                                                                                  26
 UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY (UIP)

Uncovered interest rate parity (UIP)




                         What conclusions can we draw from these relationships?




                                                                                     27
 UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY (UIP)

Implications of the UIP condition
1) Interest rate differential, ceteris paribus = expectation of exchange rate return
•   The USD return on a risk-free investment must be the same for all countries


2) Not interesting to invest in a country with higher interest rates
•   The expected depreciation is expected to offset the higher rate


3) Exchange rate coverage without cost
•   forwards contract price = expected exchange rate
•   Check that Ft,t+1 = E(St+1)!


                                                                                          28
 UNCOVERED INTEREST RATE PARITY (UIP)

Valuation model with monetary shock (textbook)
•   Determine the fundamental (long-term) value of the exchange rate PPP: E(ST )
•   Determine the current exchange rate using UIP
                                                1 + 𝑟𝐴𝑈𝐷
                                 𝑆𝑡 = 𝐸 𝑆𝑇+1             , 𝑤𝑖𝑡ℎ 𝑆 = 𝑈𝑆𝐷/𝐴𝑈𝐷
                                                1 + 𝑟𝑈𝑆𝐷


•   Analyze the impact of a US monetary shock on the exchange rate by considering
     •   the long-term effect on E(ST )
     •   the short-term effect on the interest rate rUSD



                                                                                       29
 PARITY CONDITIONS

Valuation model with monetary shock (textbook)
Impact of a money supply increase in the USA:
1. American consumers are endowed with more money.
2. Initially, all is invested in the risk-free bonds, so rUSD falls.
3. Then, they gradually use the money to buy goods.
4. So rUSD increases again and inflation slowly picks up.
5. In the long-run, all money invested in the risk-free bonds has been spent on goods.
6. So, higher consumer prices while rUSD goes back to its initial value.




                                                                                            30
 PARITY CONDITIONS

What happens if the supply of money increases in the US?

     US money supply              US price level             US interest rate




         USD/AUD
                                          Overshooting:
                                          Temporary decline in US interest
                                          rate by UIP

                                          Expected inflation effect:
                                          Rise of E(ST) by PPP
                                                                                   31
 Let’s do an exercise to better understand the mechanism!
 FORECAST OF EXCHANGE RATE DEVALUATION

Fact
60% of currencies are not floating ⇨ Important to predict a potential devaluation


Some examples of devaluations
•   November 1991: Finland, Sweden, Norway
•   September 1992: Finland, UK, Italy, Spain, Portugal
•   January 1993: Ireland
•   May 1993: Spain, Portugal
•   December 1994: Mexico
•   July 1997: Southeast Asia
•   …
                                                                                       33
 FORECAST OF EXCHANGE RATE DEVALUATION

Relevance of fundamental analysis in this case. One can use:
•   Measures of overvaluation based on PPP
•   Balance of the current account
•   Monetary growth
•   Interest rate and inflation differentials
•   Examples: Eichengreen, Rose and Wyplosz (1995), Bekaert and Gray (1998)




                                                                                 34
 FORECAST OF EXCHANGE RATE DEVALUATION

Use of interest rate parity


Example: Argentina, June 21, 2000
    •   1 ARS (Argentine Peso) = 1 USD => fixed exchange rate
    •   3-month USD interest rate in Argentina: 6.71%.
    •   3-month ARS interest rate in Argentina: 7.33%.


What is the probability of an ARS devaluation equal to a 5% increase in the exchange rate (1 to 1.05 ARS/USD)?




                                                                                                                    35
 FORECAST OF EXCHANGE RATE DEVALUATION

Use of interest rate parity
                                       𝐸 𝑆𝑡+90 = 1 − 𝑝 𝑆𝑡 + 𝑝 ∗ 1.05𝑆𝑡
with St = ARS/USD and p = probability of devaluation


Uncovered interest rate parity:
                                                            1 + 𝑟𝐴𝑅𝑆
                                             𝐸 𝑆𝑡+90   = 𝑆𝑡
                                                            1 + 𝑟𝑈𝑆𝐷
rARS= 3-month interest rate in ARS (annualized)
rUSD= 3-month interest rate in USD (annualized)



                                                                            36
 FORECAST OF EXCHANGE RATE DEVALUATION

Use of interest rate parity




Note: the interest rate difference is up to 20% in 2001! Unsurprisingly, devaluation of the peso in January 2002.




                                                                                                                       37
 FORECAST OF EXCHANGE RATE DEVALUATION

Crisis of the Argentine Peso




 Source: Economic Report, Rabobank (2013), LCU = Local Currency Unit      38
        / Introduction to international finance /




TUTOR SESSION: PPP AND PARITY CONDITIONS
 References
•   Bekaert, G., & Gray, S. F. (1998). Target zones and exchange rates: An
    empirical investigation. Journal of International Economics, 45(1), 1-35.
•   Eichengreen, B., Rose, A. K., & Wyplosz, C. (1996). Contagious currency crises.
    National Bureau of Economic Research, Working paper No. 5681.
•   Menkhoff, L., Sarno, L., Schmeling, M., & Schrimpf, A. (2017). Currency value.
    The Review of Financial Studies, 30(2), 416-441.
